funnel_fields.primary_claim: a wall of sound in your pocket.
funnel_sequence: Hero section: headline 'a wall of sound in your pocket.' + one-sentence product category descriptor + price anchor from $59 + 'visit store' CTA — establishes novelty-object identity and price accessibility in one pass

Product grid: 10 individual SKUs listed with Japanese name, category label (drum machine, synthesizer, sampler), feature bullets, price, and 'add to cart' per unit — converts category interest into individual product selection

Engineering/mechanism section: 'small device, big sound.' subhead + labeled hardware diagram + component callouts (silabs efm32 gecko mcu, cirrus logic dac, high quality speaker, high quality crystal) + design rationale for no outer case — builds product-delivers-transformation belief via hardware specificity
Ecosystem/sync section: 'combine. sync. play.' — positions individual units as modular nodes in a chainable band; drives multi-unit consideration and ecosystem lock-in
Accessories section: CA-X pro-case with feature list and 5-color availability + accessories grid (cables, headphones) — expands AOV through ecosystem accessories
Footer repeat: product family visual grid + headline repeat 'a wall of sound in your pocket.' + product descriptor repeat + from $59 + 'visit store' CTA — closes the loop back to purchase entry point
funnel_fields.offer_mechanic: Direct add-to-cart per SKU; 10 SKUs priced individually from $59 (PO-12) to $99 (PO-20, PO-28, PO-32, PO-33, PO-35); no bundle pricing, no subscription, no quantity discount visible

belief_records.execution_detail: Opens with the verbatim headline 'a wall of sound in your pocket.' — a compact noun phrase that names the core tension (big sound / small form factor) and resolves it in the product's favor before any specs are shown. The period punctuation gives it declarative finality. The phrase 'wall of sound' invokes recording-studio scale while 'in your pocket' anchors portability as the delivery mechanism. This installs the transformation belief (studio-grade sound from a pocket-sized object) as the first and only above-fold claim.
moves: Mech — format change (bare PCB as aesthetic/engineering object)
Angle — curiosity/secret (impossibly small for the claimed output)

verbatim_refs.text: a wall of sound in your pocket.

belief_records.execution_detail: Immediately below the headline, a one-sentence descriptor delivers four stacked sub-claims: 'small, ultra-portable music devices' (size/portability), 'studio quality sound' (output caliber), 'flexibility to make music on the go' (use-context freedom), 'affordable for everyone' (democratization framing). The phrase 'studio quality sound' is the strongest sub-claim — it ties pocket form factor to professional-grade output, anchoring the transformation as achievable by anyone, not just gear-owners with studio access.
moves: Angle — desire (studio-quality access without studio investment)

verbatim_refs.text: pocket operators are small, ultra-portable music devices, with studio quality sound and the flexibility to make music on the go. affordable for everyone and compatible with all other music gear.

belief_records.execution_detail: The phrase 'for an impossibly cheap price from $59' does two jobs simultaneously: the adjective 'impossibly cheap' pre-frames the price as paradoxical given the 'studio quality' claim made one sentence earlier, and the 'from $59' anchor sets a floor that invites upward product exploration. The word 'impossibly' is the load-bearing word — it pre-empts the price objection by framing the low price as the remarkable feature, not the product's limitation. This installs the worth-the-price belief before any individual product is inspected.

verbatim_refs.text: for an impossibly cheap price from

belief_records.execution_detail: Ten individual SKUs are presented in a grid: PO-12 (16 sound drum machine and sequencer, $59), PO-14 (deep bass line synthesizer, $89), PO-16 (lead synthesizer for keys melodies, $89), PO-20 (arcade synthesizer and sequencer, $99), PO-24 (noise percussion drum machine, $89), PO-28 (live synthesizer and sequencer, $99), PO-32 (drum and percussion synthesizer, $99), PO-33 (micro sampler with 40 seconds sample memory and built-in recording microphone, $99), PO-35 (vocal synthesizer and sequencer with built-in microphone for 8 different voice character sampling, $99). Each unit carries Japanese katakana naming (リズム, サブマリン, プラント, アーケード, オフィス, ロボ, トニック, ノックアウト, スピーク) plus a repeating feature pattern of 'parameter locks and punch-in effects' — shared vocabulary that signals a coherent, expert-designed system rather than random SKU proliferation. The breadth of the grid (drum machine through vocal sampler) installs belief that the transformation (music-making on the go) covers the full production chain, not just one instrument type.
moves: Mech — new feature (parameter locks, punch-in effects as proprietary vocabulary)
Market — avatar specificity (instrument-role differentiation targets different producer niches)

verbatim_refs.text: 16 sound drum machine and sequencer, perfect for making beats. parameter locks and punch-in effects.
deep bass line synthesizer with sequencer, parameter locks, play styles and punch-in effects.
micro sampler with 40 seconds sample memory and built-in recording microphone.
vocal synthesizer and sequencer with built-in microphone for 8 different voice character sampling.
the only pocket operator with unlimited sounds with microtonic vst.

belief_records.execution_detail: The section headed 'small device, big sound.' pivots from product listing to engineering explanation. The rationale is: 'by placing all vital and sensitive components under the LCD display there's no need for an outer case. by saving this cost, high quality components could be used to guarantee the best possible sound and very low power consumption. the space under the display also doubles as a speaker box.' This is a solution-UM argument — the absence of an outer case is reframed not as a cost cut but as the deliberate mechanism that funds better internal components. Specific components named: silabs efm32 gecko mcu, cirrus logic dac, high quality speaker, high quality crystal for solid clock. The labeled hardware diagram (break away hanger, JTAG programming port, sync in/audio in, built-in speaker, folding wire stand, external speaker soldering points) converts the visible bare PCB from appearing cheap to appearing intentionally engineered. This answers the implicit objection 'why is there no case?' with a mechanism claim that ties cost savings directly to audio quality.
moves: Mech — problem UM (no outer case = cost redirected to components)
Mech — new feature (external speaker soldering points, folding wire stand as hardware-hacker affordances)

verbatim_refs.text: small device, big sound.

by placing all vital and sensitive components under the LCD display there's no need for an outer case. by saving this cost, high quality components could be used to guarantee the best possible sound and very low power consumption. the space under the display also doubles as a speaker box.
silabs efm 32 gecko mcu  cirrus logic dac  high quality speaker  high quality crystal for solid clock
pocket operators are designed on a single multiple-layer  circuit board using high quality components and  all share the same compact form factor.

belief_records.execution_detail: Within the engineering section, three standalone feature lines assert portability proof: '3.5 mm SYNC IN / AUDIO IN as well as 3.5 MM AUDIO OUT / HEADPHONE SYNC OUT.' establishes pro-grade connectivity in standard format; 'portable and battery powered with 1 month battery life.' gives a concrete endurance number (1 month) that makes 'on the go' from the hero claim operational and measurable; 'compact and tactile buttons for instant feedback when you play.' adds a haptic/ergonomic claim that ties physical interaction to performance capability. Together these three lines convert the abstract 'studio quality on the go' transformation into verifiable hardware attributes.
moves: Mech — new feature (1-month battery life as portability proof)

verbatim_refs.text: portable and battery powered with 1 month battery life.
compact and tactile buttons for instant feedback when you play.
3.5 mm SYNC IN / AUDIO IN as well as 3.5 MM AUDIO OUT / HEADPHONE SYNC OUT.

belief_records.execution_detail: 'combine. sync. play.' is a three-word imperative sequence that reframes single-unit ownership as incomplete and positions multi-unit acquisition as the intended use. The supporting copy 'all pocket operator® models perfectly complement each other. from drum machine, bass and lead synthesizers to noise percussion. link and sync your pocket operators to create your own pocket band, chaining multiple pocket operators together using 3.5 mm audio cables.' deploys 'your own pocket band' as a miniaturized-studio identity frame — the buyer is not buying a gadget, they are building a band. The registered trademark symbol on 'pocket operator®' appears in this section and reinforces brand equity around the ecosystem concept. The phrase 'explore cables' CTA immediately after targets accessories upsell. This belief-unit is not in the standard 9-anchor taxonomy because it is neither pure product-delivers-transformation nor trust-the-brand — it is an ecosystem-belonging / collector-expansion belief that the single-unit is a starting node, not a terminal purchase.
moves: Market — new sub-avatar (multi-device collector/performer vs. single-unit hobbyist)
Offer — bundle (ecosystem chain as implied bundle without formal bundle pricing)

verbatim_refs.text: combine. sync. play.

all pocket operator® models perfectly complement each other. from drum machine, bass and lead synthesizers to noise percussion. link and sync your pocket operators to create your own pocket band, chaining multiple pocket operators together using 3.5 mm audio cables.
create your own pocket band

belief_records.execution_detail: 'pro-case. one size fits all.' opens the accessories section with a label ('pro-case') that elevates a silicone case into a professional-grade complement. The feature list for the CA-X case — 'hole for the microphone, anti-slip feet, neck strap, battery protection and pro-feel buttons' — does secondary work: each feature speaks to a use-context anxiety (microphone blocked, device slipping, hands-free carrying, battery damage, cheap button feel). 'Compatible with all pocket operator® models' and 'available in 5 colors' together deliver an aesthetic-collector signal (color selection implies the case is part of the object's identity expression, not just protection). 'pro-feel buttons' is notable — it names a tactile quality upgrade that implies the bare device buttons are functional but not premium, and the case resolves that. This installs a belief that the full transformation (owning a pro-grade pocket music object) is completed with the case, not just the device.
moves: Mech — new feature (pro-feel buttons as tactile upgrade)
Angle — belonging/identity (5-color selection for aesthetic expression)

verbatim_refs.text: pro-case. one size fits all.

CA-X silicone pro-case is the best companion for your pocket operator®. with an updated design, this case includes a hole for the microphone, anti-slip feet, neck strap, battery protection and pro-feel buttons. compatible with all pocket operator® models.
available in 5 colors.
pro-feel buttons

belief_records.execution_detail: The funnel closes with a full repeat of the hero section: headline 'a wall of sound in your pocket.', product descriptor, 'for an impossibly cheap price from $59', and 'visit store' CTA. The product family visual grid (drum machine / arcade synthesizer / noise percussion / micro sampler / vocal sampler categories labeled) precedes the CTA repeat. There is no countdown timer, no scarcity language, no inventory signal, no deadline. The act-now belief is attempted purely through CTA repetition and price reminder — the 'impossibly cheap' framing re-invoked at funnel close is the closest thing to an urgency construction present. Belief confidence is low because the funnel explicitly omits all standard urgency mechanics; the CTA repeat functions more as a navigation aid than a conversion trigger.
moves: Offer — price (floor anchor repeated at close)
